title: Rajendra's Fleet to Srivijaya
subtitle: When the Eastern Sea Bore the Tiger Banner
era: Imperial Chola naval expedition, c. 1025 CE
region: Bay of Bengal, Srivijaya (Sumatra) and Kadaram (Malaya)
moral: Vision dares where others dream, yet power reached far must be held with wisdom.

sources.citation: Rajendra's Tanjore inscription listing the Southeast Asian ports (Kadaram, Sri Vijaya)
George Coedes, The Indianized States of Southeast Asia

panels.shot: epic low-angle wide of a royal flagship cutting across a moonlit ocean

dialogue.text: Rajendra Chola, lord of the Kaveri, sends a tiger-bannered armada across the rising eastern sea, to strike distant Srivijaya in the year of our Lord around 1025.

panels.shot: wide interior of a temple hall, slightly low-angle, emphasizing grandeur

dialogue.text: In Thanjavur’s stone womb of power, Rajendra, son of Rajaraja, bows before Shiva. Behind him, the Chola court waits for his next move after victories on land.

panels.shot: medium close-up side profile of Rajendra, contemplative, with map in foreground

dialogue.text: His armies have carried the tiger standard to the Ganga. Yet across the sea, Srivijaya holds the straits and tolls Chola trade, weighing his empire’s throat with unseen hands.

panels.shot: wide council-hall shot, elevated vantage, showing ministers and commanders around

dialogue.text: Merchants murmur of seized cargoes and slighted envoys. Courtiers speak of prestige, rivals and routes. The sea, once a highway of pilgrims and pepper, has become a field of contest.

panels.shot: tight three-quarter view focusing on Rajendra rising from throne, commanding presence

dialogue.speaker: rajendra_chola_i
dialogue.text: If the straits choke our traders, we will open them with our oars.

panels.shot: sweeping bird's-eye over a busy Kaveri river wharf, teeming with ships and workers

dialogue.text: From Nagapattinam to Kaveripattinam, shipyards awaken. Timber forests march into hulls, copper into nails, sailors from coasts and islands into ordered crews under the Chola seal.

panels.shot: medium shot on Rajendra inspecting a flagship at dockside, slight low-angle

dialogue.text: This is no coastal raid. It is an armada: long fighting ships with ranks of oars, high-sided transports for horses and men, supply craft heavy with grain, water and iron.

panels.shot: dramatic wide shot from behind Rajendra, looking out to sea at assembled fleet at dawn

dialogue.text: The Bay of Bengal lies calm, but the horizon is crowded with masts. No Indian emperor before has gathered such force upon the water.

panels.shot: close-up on Rajendra’s hand raising a conch as men watch tensely

dialogue.speaker: rajendra_chola_i
dialogue.text: For trade, for honour, for the name of Chola—row east.

panels.shot: high wide shot of the fleet leaving the Coromandel coast, coastline receding

dialogue.text: Oars bite water in unison. Sails billow with monsoon winds. The fleet bears warriors, interpreters, pilots who know foreign currents, and the weight of a king’s decision.

panels.shot: medium interior shot on Rajendra in the low-roofed captain’s cabin during storm

dialogue.text: The eastern sea tests them with squalls, unseen sandbars and starless nights. Yet pilots read swells and winds, tracing routes long walked by merchants and monks.

panels.shot: wide shot of fleet approaching a foreign island coast fringed with forests

dialogue.text: First come islands and lesser ports: waystations that mark the long arc toward Sumatra and the Malay coast, where Srivijaya’s power rests on harbours and river mouths.

panels.shot: medium shot from behind Rajendra as he watches Srivijayan harbor in the distance

dialogue.text: Inscriptions at his capital will later remember these names: Sri Vijaya, Kadaram, and other ports, bound not by temples alone but by tolls, warehouses and armed quays.

panels.shot: dynamic wide shot of Chola ships closing in on a fortified harbor, angled from above

dialogue.text: Battle comes swift by water. Chola ships drive into harbours, break chains and barriers, seize anchorages. The blow is aimed at power and revenue, not at empty boasting.

panels.shot: medium shot on Rajendra on deck amidst ordered chaos, gesturing commands

dialogue.text: Srivijaya, master of straits and entrepôts, is struck in its heartlands. Coedes would later write of an Indian king whose fleet cut through the island empire’s delicate web of control.

panels.shot: wide shot of Chola troops disembarking at a Malay port, jungle in background

dialogue.text: At Kadaram, gateway of the Malay Peninsula, his men come ashore. They do not stay to rule its jungles, but to show that the sea-roads cannot be closed to the Kaveri.

panels.shot: medium shot aboard ship showing captured foreign envoys and merchants under guard

dialogue.text: Ports fall, allies are sought, rivals warned. The expedition is a message: that the bay is not Srivijaya’s lake alone, and that distant kings must account for southern interests.

panels.shot: solemn wide shot of the fleet turning homeward at sea, with foreign coastline fading

dialogue.text: Then, as swiftly as it came, the armada withdraws. Rajendra’s goal is struck: prestige won, trade routes shaken, Srivijaya’s aura of untouchable sea-power cracked.

panels.shot: ceremonial wide shot inside a Chola temple hall, focus on inscription being carved

dialogue.text: In Rajendra’s Tanjore inscription, the captured or humbled ports are carved in stone. Stone outlives storm and voyage; it tells posterity that an Indian fleet once carried war beyond India’s shores.

panels.shot: medium close-up of an older Rajendra sitting reflective in his palace garden at dusk

dialogue.text: He has bathed his name in the Ganga, sent his flag to Malay seas. Yet even for him, the eastern ocean remains a road of influence, not a field of lasting rule.

panels.shot: final wide shot of the Bay of Bengal at sunrise with faint outline of temples and ships

dialogue.text: Rajendra’s armada shows how vision and ships can carry a kingdom’s reach across horizons. Yet it also whispers that power flung far must be guided by wisdom, lest waves erase its marks.
